title: Myasthenic vs Cholinergic Crisis
subtitle: Neuromuscular Respiratory Failure & the Tensilon Differential

patient.name: P. Donnelly
patient.room: ED Acute Bed 4, neurology + MICU evaluating

labs.r: More negative than −60 normal; intubate if less negative than −20 to −30
60–80 mL/kg normal; intubate if <15 mL/kg or <1 L

labs.n: Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) binding antibody — known from prior workup

labs.v: Bibasilar plate-like atelectasis; lung fields otherwise clear; no consolidation or pulmonary edema

labs.n: CT sinus (from urgent care 5 days ago)
labs.v: Mild bilateral maxillary mucosal thickening; no air-fluid levels; no acute findings
FAILED — nasal regurgitation with 5 mL water; wet, gurgly voice quality after sip
labs.r: Passes 30 mL water trial without choking or wet voice

labs.v: 4 mm bilateral, briskly reactive (NORMAL — no miosis)
No excessive salivation, lacrimation, diaphoresis. No abdominal cramping, urinary urgency, or diarrhea. No fasciculations.

nursesNote: 1410 — 64 y/o female with an 8-year history of generalized seropositive (AChR-Ab +) myasthenia gravis, normally well-controlled on pyridostigmine 60 mg PO QID, prednisone 20 mg PO daily, and mycophenolate mofetil 1,000 mg PO BID. Followed in our neurology clinic; last clinic visit 1 month ago was at her baseline (NIF −75 cm H2O, FVC 3.2 L, no bulbar symptoms, fully ambulatory and working part-time as a librarian). Husband reports she developed nasal congestion and mild facial pressure 6 days ago and was seen at urgent care. Started on AZITHROMYCIN 500 mg PO day 1, then 250 mg daily x 4 days (Z-Pak) for presumed bacterial sinusitis — neither the urgent care provider nor the client recognized that macrolide antibiotics can precipitate myasthenic crisis. The patient did not call her neurologist before filling the antibiotic. Over the last 4 days she has developed progressively worsening ptosis (bilateral, worse late in the day), diplopia on lateral gaze, generalized fatigability, a soft and nasal-sounding voice, and difficulty swallowing — to the point that this morning she could NOT swallow her 0800 pyridostigmine dose without choking. She has not taken pyridostigmine since the 2300 dose last night. Husband became alarmed when he found her struggling to breathe on the couch and brought her to the ED. On assessment: alert, anxious, sitting upright on the gurney, dyspneic with paradoxical abdominal breathing pattern, speaking in short broken phrases with a softly nasal voice; bilateral ptosis worse on the left; weak neck flexion (unable to lift head off bed against gravity); shoulder shrug weak (3/5 bilaterally); proximal lower extremity weakness (3/5 hip flexion); distal strength preserved. CRITICALLY for the differential: pupils are 4 mm and briskly reactive (NO miosis), there is NO excessive salivation, NO lacrimation, NO diaphoresis, NO urinary urgency, NO diarrhea, NO abdominal cramping, NO muscle fasciculations, and NO bradycardia — i.e., absent SLUDGE/B symptoms. Bedside swallow screen FAILED — nasal regurgitation with 5 mL of water and a wet, gurgly voice quality. NIF has dropped from a baseline of −75 cm H2O to −18 cm H2O. FVC is 0.9 L (≈14 mL/kg). ABG shows type II respiratory failure (pH 7.30, PaCO2 54, PaO2 64 on 4 L NC). Pyridostigmine level sent but not back. NPO; neurology and MICU at bedside. Clinical picture: MYASTHENIC CRISIS precipitated by macrolide antibiotic exposure and missed maintenance pyridostigmine, with imminent respiratory failure and high aspiration risk from bulbar weakness.
providerOrders: Strict NPO (bulbar weakness with failed swallow screen — high aspiration risk)
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO2
DISCONTINUE azithromycin immediately; document on the chart as a 'myasthenia-precipitating drug — avoid future use'
Bedside NIF and FVC q2h, more frequently if trending down
If NIF less negative than −20 OR FVC <15 mL/kg OR clinical paradoxical breathing/bulbar incompetence: prepare for intubation
Avoid succinylcholine (prolonged duration in MG); use rocuronium at REDUCED dose if RSI required; have neostigmine + sugammadex available
Avoid BiPAP if bulbar weakness is severe (cannot protect airway) — intubate directly if respiratory failure

Edrophonium (Tensilon) test if diagnosis uncertain (myasthenic vs cholinergic): 2 mg IV test dose; if no adverse reaction in 1 min, give 8 mg IV (total 10 mg). Atropine 0.5–1 mg IV at bedside in pre-drawn syringe (treats bradycardia/cholinergic adverse effects).
HOLD pyridostigmine pending Tensilon result if cholinergic crisis cannot be confidently excluded
Initiate PLASMAPHERESIS (5 exchanges over 7–10 days) OR IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days — neurology to choose
Continue prednisone 20 mg PO daily (do not abruptly stop chronic immunosuppression); consider IV methylprednisolone if NPO and worsening
Hold mycophenolate during acute infection workup; resume when stable per neurology
Aspiration precautions: HOB elevated 30°, oral suction at bedside
DVT prophylaxis: sequential compression devices; enoxaparin if not bleeding risk
Family teaching once stable: list of myasthenia-precipitating drugs and the importance of contacting the MG neurologist before starting any new medication

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: NIF dropped from −75 cm H2O (baseline) to −18 cm H2O; FVC 0.9 L (≈14 mL/kg)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the most important objective measurements of neuromuscular respiratory failure. The 'rule of 20-30-40' for impending intubation in MG: FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −30 cm H2O, MEP <40 cm H2O. This client meets ALL THREE — intubation should be planned NOW, not when she crashes.

opts.text: ABG: pH 7.30, PaCO2 54, PaO2 64 on 4 L NC — type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. PaCO2 elevation indicates the respiratory muscles can no longer maintain alveolar ventilation. This is FATIGUE, not 'just hypoxia.' In neuromuscular disease, hypercapnia is a late sign — by the time CO2 rises, respiratory arrest is close.

opts.text: Failed bedside swallow screen with nasal regurgitation of water and wet, gurgly voice after sip
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — bulbar weakness. Bulbar dysfunction means the client cannot protect her airway. Strict NPO. Aspiration is a major cause of morbidity in MG crisis — and one of the strongest indications to intubate EARLY, even before NIF/FVC reach the absolute thresholds.

opts.text: Paradoxical abdominal breathing (chest moving in during inspiration) and accessory muscle use
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diaphragmatic fatigue. The diaphragm normally pulls the abdomen out during inspiration. When it fails, the abdomen retracts inward (paradoxical movement). Accessory muscle recruitment is a temporary compensation — those muscles also fatigue, and the client is now using them just to breathe at rest.

opts.text: Azithromycin (Z-Pak) started 5 days ago at urgent care — a macrolide antibiotic — with progressive weakness developing over the same period
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — IDENTIFIES THE PRECIPITANT. Macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin), aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin), fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin), beta-blockers, magnesium, certain anesthetics, and quinine all impair neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate myasthenic crisis. DISCONTINUE the offending drug and flag it as 'avoid lifelong.'

opts.text: Missed her 0800 pyridostigmine dose because she could no longer swallow it; last dose was 2300 the night before
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — undermedication contributing. Pyridostigmine half-life is ~3–4 hours. By the time she reached the ED, she had been without her AChE inhibitor for ~14 hours. Missing the drug combined with the macrolide trigger is the two-hit combination that produced this crisis.

opts.text: Bilateral ptosis, weak neck flexion, weak shoulder shrug (3/5), proximal lower extremity weakness (3/5)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the classic generalized MG distribution. Ocular, bulbar, neck flexor, and proximal limb weakness with relative sparing of distal strength. The 'fatigability pattern' — strength worsens with repeated effort and improves with rest.

opts.text: Pupils 4 mm and briskly reactive (NO miosis); no excessive salivation, lacrimation, urinary urgency, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, fasciculations, or bradycardia — i.e., ABSENT SLUDGE/B symptoms
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these PERTINENT NEGATIVES help distinguish MYASTHENIC from CHOLINERGIC crisis. SLUDGE = Salivation, Lacrimation, Urinary incontinence, Diarrhea, GI cramping/Emesis; plus B for bronchospasm/bradycardia. The mnemonic 'DUMBBELS' or 'SLUDGE' captures muscarinic excess from too much pyridostigmine. Their ABSENCE here makes myasthenic crisis (undermedication + drug trigger) much more likely than cholinergic crisis (overmedication).

opts.text: Speaking in short broken phrases with a soft, nasal voice quality
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Nasal voice quality indicates weakness of the palatal musculature (bulbar dysfunction); short phrases reflect inadequate breath support. Both worsen aspiration and ventilatory failure risk. The bedside speech pattern is one of the most sensitive early markers of crisis progression.

opts.text: Temperature 99.8°F
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue in isolation. Low-grade temperature is nonspecific — can occur with increased work of breathing alone. No leukocytosis, no localizing infectious signs other than the resolving sinusitis. Document but not actionable.

opts.text: Sodium 138, potassium 4.0, magnesium 2.0
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue — values are normal. However, magnesium specifically deserves a 'no IV mag, ever' flag in the MG chart: IV magnesium impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate crisis. Normal serum Mg is fine; therapeutic IV magnesium boluses are CONTRAINDICATED.

opts.text: Bibasilar plate-like atelectasis on chest X-ray
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue. Mild atelectasis is expected with shallow breathing and reduced cough strength. Will improve with respiratory support and improved tidal volumes. Not a sign of consolidation/pneumonia and does not change immediate management.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Antibodies against the post-synaptic acetylcholine receptor reduce available receptors at the neuromuscular junction; this client's normally adequate ACh release now fails to depolarize the muscle with repetitive effort — producing fatigability that worsens later in the day
opts.rat: CORRECT — the central pathophysiology. AChR-Ab binding causes receptor internalization and complement-mediated NMJ damage. With each successive nerve impulse, the rundown of available receptors produces decremental muscle response (the same finding seen on repetitive nerve stimulation EMG). Hence: stronger in the morning, weaker through the day.

opts.text: Macrolides (and aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, IV magnesium) impair neuromuscular transmission by interfering with ACh release or postsynaptic function — DISCONTINUE the offending agent at once
opts.rat: CORRECT and a critical clinical decision. Mechanisms vary (macrolides have direct curare-like postsynaptic effects; aminoglycosides reduce presynaptic ACh release; magnesium competes with calcium at presynaptic terminals) but the common result is impaired NMJ function in a client already running on reduced receptor reserve. ALL MG clients should have an 'avoid' list posted at the bedside and in their chart.

opts.text: Myasthenic crisis (UNDERmedication / trigger): weakness from too little ACh effect; absent SLUDGE; pupils normal/dilated; Tensilon IMPROVES strength; treatment is plasmapheresis or IVIG + maintenance pyridostigmine
opts.rat: CORRECT — the myasthenic side of the differential. Insufficient ACh action at the NMJ from antibody-mediated receptor depletion plus precipitants. Edrophonium (Tensilon) — a short-acting AChE inhibitor — provides transient additional ACh, producing measurable improvement in ptosis, neck flexion, or grip strength within 1–2 minutes.

opts.text: Cholinergic crisis (OVERmedication): weakness from excessive ACh saturating and desensitizing the NMJ; PRESENT SLUDGE symptoms; miosis; fasciculations; bradycardia; Tensilon WORSENS strength; treatment is HOLD pyridostigmine, atropine for muscarinic symptoms, supportive care
opts.rat: CORRECT — the cholinergic side. Excess ACh from too-frequent or too-high pyridostigmine doses initially overstimulates and then desensitizes nicotinic receptors. The muscarinic 'side effects' (SLUDGE) precede or accompany the nicotinic weakness. Atropine blocks the muscarinic effects but does NOT reverse the nicotinic weakness — that simply has to wash out with time.

opts.text: In neuromuscular respiratory failure, HYPERCAPNIA is a LATE sign — by the time PaCO2 rises, respiratory arrest is close; clinicians must intubate based on TRENDS in NIF/FVC and bulbar function, NOT wait for ABG to be 'bad enough'
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield teaching point. Unlike COPD where hypercapnia is a chronic compensated baseline, in MG crisis a rising CO2 reflects diaphragmatic fatigue and impending arrest. The bedside metrics (NIF, FVC, bulbar exam) detect failure HOURS before the ABG does. Intubation in MG is a deliberate, anticipated, controlled event — never wait for the crash.

opts.text: Severe bulbar weakness with failed swallow screen and nasal regurgitation creates HIGH aspiration risk; BiPAP may not be appropriate when airway protection is compromised — direct intubation is often safer
opts.rat: CORRECT. BiPAP is useful in selected MG clients with respiratory muscle fatigue BUT intact bulbar function and protected airway. With this client's failed swallow and pooled secretions, BiPAP risks aspiration of gastric contents under positive pressure. Intubation provides definitive airway protection and assisted ventilation.

opts.text: This client's clinical picture (absent SLUDGE, recent pyridostigmine miss, macrolide trigger, normal pupils, no fasciculations) strongly favors MYASTHENIC crisis over cholinergic crisis
opts.rat: CORRECT — the bedside differential. Each pertinent negative is part of the picture: normal pupils argue against cholinergic; absent SLUDGE argues against cholinergic; recent macrolide and missed pyridostigmine doses argue FOR myasthenic. If clinical picture were ambiguous, a Tensilon test would resolve it — but here the clinical picture is fairly clear.

opts.text: Succinylcholine should be used at standard doses for rapid sequence intubation in this client
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and dangerous. MG clients have UNPREDICTABLE responses to neuromuscular blocking drugs: resistant to succinylcholine due to reduced receptors but PROLONGED depolarization once given; markedly SENSITIVE to non-depolarizing agents (rocuronium, vecuronium) with greatly extended duration. If RSI is required, use rocuronium at REDUCED dose and have sugammadex available for reversal.

opts.text: IV magnesium sulfate should be given routinely to all MG clients in crisis for neuroprotection
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — DIRECTLY CONTRAINDICATED. Magnesium competes with calcium at the presynaptic terminal, reducing ACh release. IV magnesium can precipitate or worsen MG crisis. Some MG client charts post a 'NO IV MAGNESIUM' alert. Even oral magnesium-containing laxatives should be reviewed with the neurologist.

opts.text: Plasmapheresis works by removing the AChR antibodies from circulation; IVIG works by saturating Fc receptors and modulating the immune response — BOTH are short-term bridges that allow steroids and chronic immunosuppression to take effect over weeks
opts.rat: CORRECT. Plasmapheresis (5 exchanges over 7–10 days) removes circulating pathogenic antibodies — fastest onset (improvement within days) but technically demanding and requires good vascular access. IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) has equivalent efficacy with a different mechanism, no need for central line, and useful when plasmapheresis is unavailable. Choice depends on local availability and patient factors.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Impaired Spontaneous Ventilation r/t neuromuscular weakness with diaphragmatic fatigue (NIF −18, FVC 14 mL/kg, paradoxical breathing, PaCO2 54)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Breathing (ABCs). The client is in established type II respiratory failure with all three '20-30-40' criteria met. Intubation is anticipated and should occur before respiratory arrest, NOT after.

opts.text: Risk for Aspiration r/t bulbar weakness with failed swallow, nasal regurgitation, and pooling secretions
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Airway. Bulbar weakness compromises the client's ability to protect her airway. Strict NPO, HOB elevated 30°, oral suction at bedside. Bulbar failure with poor secretion clearance is one of the strongest single indications to intubate even if NIF/FVC have not crossed thresholds.

opts.text: Need to Differentiate Myasthenic vs Cholinergic Crisis (treatment is opposite for each)
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical decision. Treatment differs fundamentally: myasthenic = MORE ACh effect (pyridostigmine + immunomodulation); cholinergic = LESS ACh effect (HOLD pyridostigmine + atropine for muscarinic symptoms). Mistake either direction worsens the patient. Clinical picture + Tensilon test if needed.

opts.text: Decreased Mobility / Activity Intolerance r/t generalized neuromuscular weakness (proximal 3/5, neck flexion 3/5)
opts.rat: #4 — Real but not immediately life-threatening. Bed rest with assistance; range-of-motion, pressure injury prevention, DVT prophylaxis. Strength will improve as immunomodulation takes effect. Early mobilization once weaned from ventilator.

opts.text: Deficient Knowledge r/t MG-precipitating drugs and the importance of contacting the neurologist before starting any new medication
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment, but THE preventable factor for the NEXT crisis. The urgent care visit that started this whole admission is the perfect teaching example. List of dangerous drugs (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, IV magnesium, certain anesthetics), MedicAlert bracelet, and a 'call MG neurologist before any new med' rule.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Discontinue azithromycin immediately and document on the chart and home medication list as a 'myasthenia-precipitating drug — avoid'
opts.rat: Removes the active precipitant. Most importantly, prevents future prescribers (urgent care, dentist, ED) from making the same error. Many institutions use an EMR allergy/intolerance flag specifically for 'MG-precipitating drugs.'

opts.text: Strict NPO with HOB elevated 30°, oral suction at bedside
opts.rat: Aspiration precautions in a client with failed swallow and pooling secretions. Even small aspiration events in a neuromuscular patient with weak cough can produce dramatic pneumonia and worsen the crisis.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO2, and serial NIF/FVC every 2 hours (more frequently if trending down)
opts.rat: Bedside trends drive the intubation decision. The single biggest mistake in MG crisis is waiting for the ABG or for respiratory arrest. NIF/FVC are checked by the respiratory therapist and decline is a clear indication to plan intubation EARLY.

opts.text: Prepare for intubation now: anticipated (not crash) intubation given NIF −18, FVC 0.9 L, paradoxical breathing, and failed swallow
opts.rat: All three '20-30-40' criteria are met PLUS bulbar incompetence. Anticipated intubation in a controlled setting with experienced operator and full pre-oxygenation is vastly safer than a crash intubation after respiratory arrest in a client with already-compromised airway protection.

opts.text: If rapid sequence intubation needed: use rocuronium at REDUCED dose (avoid succinylcholine in MG); have sugammadex available for reversal; atropine and standard resuscitation drugs at bedside
opts.rat: MG clients are markedly sensitive to non-depolarizing NMBDs. Reduced doses produce normal blockade with prolonged duration; standard doses produce prolonged paralysis. Sugammadex selectively reverses rocuronium and is the preferred reversal agent. Succinylcholine is avoided — unpredictable response.

opts.text: Edrophonium (Tensilon) test ONLY if the myasthenic vs cholinergic differential cannot be made clinically: 2 mg IV test dose; if no adverse reaction at 1 min, give 8 mg IV (total 10 mg). Atropine 0.5–1 mg IV pre-drawn at bedside.
opts.rat: Reasonable diagnostic intervention when the clinical picture is ambiguous. Tensilon (edrophonium) is a short-acting AChE inhibitor (~5 min duration). IMPROVES strength → myasthenic crisis; WORSENS or no change → cholinergic crisis. Have atropine at bedside in case of severe muscarinic adverse effects (bradycardia, bronchospasm, severe salivation). NOTE: many centers now skip the Tensilon test and rely on history + clinical picture; it is becoming less commonly performed but remains testable NCLEX content.

opts.text: Initiate plasmapheresis (5 exchanges over 7–10 days) OR IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days — neurology to choose based on local availability and patient factors
opts.rat: Both are first-line immunomodulating treatments for myasthenic crisis. Plasmapheresis: fastest onset (days), requires central venous access, can be hemodynamically demanding. IVIG: equivalent efficacy, no need for central line, monitor for headache/aseptic meningitis/renal injury/thromboembolism. Either is appropriate.

opts.text: Continue prednisone 20 mg PO daily (or IV methylprednisolone if NPO) — do NOT abruptly stop chronic immunosuppression
opts.rat: Abrupt cessation of chronic steroids risks adrenal insufficiency AND removes baseline immunosuppression. Continue at current dose; some centers ADD high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1 g IV daily x 3–5 days) — though this can transiently WORSEN MG strength in the first week, which is why plasmapheresis/IVIG is given concurrently. Coordinate with neurology.

opts.text: Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV bolus to 'protect the airway and prevent seizures'
opts.rat: ⚠️ DIRECTLY CONTRAINDICATED. IV magnesium impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate or worsen MG crisis. There is no indication for it here (no eclampsia, no severe asthma, no torsades). This is the kind of order a covering provider might write without realizing the MG context — the bedside nurse should QUESTION it before administering.

opts.text: Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV q24h empirically for aspiration pneumonia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Aminoglycosides are CONTRAINDICATED in MG (reduce presynaptic ACh release → worsen weakness). The chest X-ray does not show consolidation, and there is no clinical evidence of pneumonia. Even if pneumonia were present, MG-safe alternatives exist (ceftriaxone, vancomycin, linezolid, ampicillin-sulbactam). Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) are ALSO contraindicated.

opts.text: Increase pyridostigmine to 120 mg PO every 2 hours empirically to overcome the weakness
opts.rat: Risky without first excluding cholinergic crisis. If cholinergic crisis IS the cause, more pyridostigmine worsens the weakness AND the muscarinic symptoms. Even in confirmed myasthenic crisis, dose escalation should be modest, deliberate, and after immunomodulation has begun. Also: this client is NPO with failed swallow — oral pyridostigmine cannot be safely given right now.

opts.text: BiPAP 12/5 cm H2O as definitive ventilatory support — avoid intubation
opts.rat: Wrong choice with significant bulbar weakness and failed swallow. BiPAP forces air under pressure and can drive gastric contents into the airway. With this client's failed swallow and pooling secretions, intubation (which protects the airway) is safer. BiPAP CAN be used in selected MG clients with respiratory muscle fatigue but intact bulbar function — not this one.

opts.text: Discharge teaching once stable: MG-precipitating drug list (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, IV magnesium, certain anesthetics), MedicAlert bracelet identifying MG, rule to contact MG neurologist BEFORE starting any new medication (including over-the-counter)
opts.rat: The single highest-leverage preventive intervention. Most MG crises are precipitated by medication, infection, or noncompliance. A written drug list carried in the wallet, an MG MedicAlert bracelet, and a clear rule about new medications prevents the urgent-care-style trigger that produced this admission.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Strict NPO; HOB 30°; oral suction at bedside; discontinue azithromycin; continuous cardiac and end-tidal CO2 monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate safety measures requiring no orders or delay: protect the airway (NPO + positioning + suction), remove the active trigger (azithromycin), and detect ventilatory failure in real time (ETCO2 rises before SpO2 falls in neuromuscular weakness).

opts.text: Call neurology + MICU; prepare for anticipated intubation with rocuronium-reduced-dose RSI; have sugammadex, atropine, and standard resuscitation drugs at bedside; pre-oxygenate the client
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Anticipated intubation in a controlled setting is far safer than crash intubation after arrest. Pre-oxygenation, experienced operator, and the right drugs (NOT succinylcholine, reduced rocuronium, sugammadex available) make the difference between a controlled airway and an aspiration disaster.

opts.text: If myasthenic vs cholinergic crisis is clinically clear (as in this client — absent SLUDGE, recent macrolide trigger, missed pyridostigmine): skip the Tensilon test; if ambiguous, perform Tensilon (2 mg test → 8 mg if no AE) with atropine pre-drawn at bedside
opts.rat: #3 — Diagnostic decision. The bedside differential is straightforward in this client. In ambiguous cases, Tensilon — short-acting AChE inhibitor — is given as 2 mg IV test dose with continuous monitoring; if no adverse reaction in 1 minute, the remaining 8 mg is pushed. Strength improves in myasthenic crisis (≈80% of patients show clear improvement) and worsens or stays the same in cholinergic crisis.

opts.text: Initiate plasmapheresis OR IVIG (neurology to choose); continue prednisone; coordinate with hospital pharmacy and dialysis/apheresis team
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive immunomodulating treatment. Plasmapheresis removes antibodies; IVIG modulates the immune response. Both produce improvement within days. Onset is fast enough that improvements may be visible during the same hospitalization. Maintenance immunosuppression (prednisone ± steroid-sparing agents) continues for long-term control.

opts.text: Once stable and extubated: bedside teaching on MG-precipitating drug list, MedicAlert bracelet, and rule to contact MG neurologist before any new medication; arrange neurology follow-up in 1–2 weeks
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management and prevention. This is what prevents the NEXT crisis. The macrolide that started this admission would not have been prescribed if the urgent care had known — or if the client had been carrying a written list and had said 'I have MG, here is my drug list.' Patient empowerment is the single most effective long-term intervention.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 3 after plasmapheresis exchange #2: NIF improved from −18 to −42 cm H2O, FVC 1.8 L, swallow screen now passes 30 mL water, no nasal regurgitation, extubated to room air with SpO2 96%
opts.rat: Classic plasmapheresis response. Antibody removal allows the remaining functional AChR receptors to support adequate neuromuscular transmission. Bulbar function returns; respiratory mechanics recover. Pyridostigmine PO restarted at maintenance dose. Continue exchanges per neurology (typically 5 total).

opts.text: Day 7: completed 5 plasmapheresis exchanges; ambulating with PT; full diet; pyridostigmine 60 mg PO QID + prednisone 20 mg PO daily resumed; MG-precipitating drug list and MedicAlert bracelet completed; discharge home with neurology follow-up in 1 week
opts.rat: Successful disposition trajectory. The teaching component is critical — discharge with written drug list, bracelet, and a clear plan to contact MG neurologist before any new prescription. Many MG centers also provide a wallet card listing dangerous medications.

opts.text: If Tensilon test had been performed: 2 mg IV produced clear improvement in ptosis and neck flexion within 60 seconds — full 8 mg dose then given with sustained 5-minute improvement
opts.rat: Strongly supports MYASTHENIC crisis. The transient improvement confirms the diagnosis and supports proceeding with immunomodulation (plasmapheresis or IVIG) plus eventual resumption of pyridostigmine at appropriate maintenance dose.

opts.text: 1 hour post-admission, before intubation: NIF drops from −18 to −10; client becomes diaphoretic, more obtunded, RR drops from 32 to 8 with shallow breaths, SpO2 75% on 4 L NC
opts.rat: EMERGENT — respiratory arrest is imminent. Immediate bag-mask ventilation, call code/RRT, urgent intubation with reduced-dose rocuronium (NOT succinylcholine). This is exactly why anticipated intubation BEFORE this point is the standard of care in MG crisis.

opts.text: Tensilon test produces dramatic worsening: 30 seconds after 2 mg IV the client develops salivation, diaphoresis, bradycardia to 42, miosis, and increased weakness — atropine 0.5 mg IV given with resolution of bradycardia and muscarinic symptoms
opts.rat: This is CHOLINERGIC crisis (the alternative diagnosis). HOLD pyridostigmine entirely; supportive care; atropine for muscarinic symptoms. The nicotinic weakness will resolve as pyridostigmine washes out over 24–48 hours. Re-evaluate pyridostigmine dosing once recovered. This case demonstrates why atropine MUST be pre-drawn at the bedside before any Tensilon test.

opts.text: Post-intubation day 2: develops new fever 102°F, increased purulent endotracheal secretions, new right lower lobe infiltrate on CXR, WBC 16 K with bandemia
opts.rat: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). Obtain endotracheal aspirate culture; start MG-SAFE antibiotics (ceftriaxone, ampicillin-sulbactam, vancomycin, linezolid). AVOID aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin) and fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin). VAP can precipitate prolonged crisis — aggressive treatment is essential.

opts.text: Day 4 of IVIG (after total dose 1.6 g/kg given): new severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia; LP shows mildly elevated WBC with normal glucose and protein; cultures negative
opts.rat: Aseptic meningitis — a known IVIG complication, typically self-limited. Other IVIG adverse effects to monitor for: anaphylaxis (especially in IgA-deficient patients), acute kidney injury (older sucrose-stabilized products), thromboembolism (especially in older patients with vascular risk factors). Symptomatic management; resolves within days. Future IVIG infusions: slower rate, pre-medication.

opts.text: Bilateral mild ptosis persists at end of day on day 5 despite plasmapheresis, but is markedly improved from admission and present only with sustained upgaze >30 sec
opts.rat: Expected residual finding. Many MG clients have mild fluctuating ptosis at their baseline. The clinically important fact is the magnitude relative to baseline (markedly improved) and the absence of bulbar/respiratory involvement. Ptosis with sustained upgaze is the bedside fatigability test.

opts.text: Day 6: client tells you 'I think I'll skip the MedicAlert bracelet, my husband will remember to tell future doctors I have MG'
opts.rat: Knowledge deficit unresolved. Husbands are not always present (work, errands, separate ED visits). The bracelet is durable, always-on documentation that anesthesiology, EMS, and ED providers will see during an emergency. Re-teach; consider involving social work or the MG support group; emphasize the cost of THIS admission was a single urgent-care prescription that the bracelet might have prevented.

opts.text: Persistent positive AChR-Ab titer (now 14.8 nmol/L on discharge labs) consistent with her known underlying generalized MG
opts.rat: Expected. AChR-Ab levels don't track tightly with disease severity in individuals and can persist for years on treatment. The clinically relevant marker is symptoms + respiratory mechanics, not the titer. Long-term immunosuppression (prednisone + steroid-sparing agent) aims for symptomatic remission, not antibody clearance.
